summary:
The Impressed Ware culture, sometimes grouped with or seen as a precursor or variant of the [[event:1422|Cardial Pottery culture]], was a Neolithic ceramic and cultural tradition of the central and western Mediterranean, dated from approximately 6500 to 5500 BCE. The culture is defined by its distinctive pottery decorated by pressing various implements — shells, sticks, fingers, and combs — into the wet clay before firing to produce impressed geometric patterns. This pottery tradition marks the arrival of farming communities along the Mediterranean coastlines of Italy, southern France, and beyond.

The Impressed Ware tradition represents part of the maritime spread of early Neolithic farming communities along the Mediterranean littoral, a route of agricultural expansion distinct from the overland Danubian route through the Balkans. These coastal Neolithic communities appear to have moved by boat, establishing farming settlements along coastlines and islands before progressing inland. This maritime colonization strategy allowed farming to spread rapidly across a long geographic arc from the eastern to the western Mediterranean in just a few centuries.

The economy of Impressed Ware communities was based on herding — particularly sheep and goats — alongside some cereal agriculture, and the exploitation of marine resources from coastal locations. The cultural assemblage, including pottery forms, stone tool types, and architectural practices, shows both common features across the Mediterranean distribution and regional variation reflecting local adaptations. This culture represents the initial Neolithization of much of the central Mediterranean, setting the stage for the subsequent cultural elaboration of the European Neolithic.
